content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
{916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Nellie Ancel 
Senior Deputy Attorney 
City of Fremont 
3300 Capitol Avenue 
Fremont, CA 94537 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-173 
Dear Ms. Ancel: 
October 12, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et 
seq. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law other than Government Code Section 
1090 and the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We are also not a finder of fact when rendering 
advice (/11 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice \Ve provide assumes your facts arc 
complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section I 090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Alameda County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Fremont from contracting with a real estate broker 
who will represent the city as both the potential buyer and seller (or lessee and lessor) in real 
property transactions? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Where a real estate broker has not advised or influenced the public body prior to the 
contract under which it now seeks to provide technical expertise in facilitating the acquisition or 
sale of real property, Section 1090 is not applicable. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 IO00 through 9 I 014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-173 
Page No. 2 
The City of Fremont {the "City") is planning to construct a new civic center on a site already 
owned by the City. In preparation for this process, the City plans on leasing or purchasing 
additional property to be used for the temporary relocation of several city departments. 
In 2012, the City adopted the Civic Center Master Plan, as a part of its plan for a 
comprehensive redevelopment of Downtown. The City has identified a 5.7-acre site for the Civic 
Center, which will include a large public plaza, city administrative offices and other potential 
community-focused uses, as well as adjacent parking facilities for staff, visitors and nearby 
businesses. The Civic Center is envisioned to include all City departments and functions except 
Police, Detention, Animal Control and Maintenance, which functions are located off site. The Civic 
Center site is currently partially occupied by the City's Family Resource Center, which is to be 
integrated into the master plan. 
Development of the Civic Center's administrative building would displace the Family 
Resource Center, currently located within two buildings at the site. The City is in the process of 
preparing a transition plan that would relocate the Family Resources Center either to a new property 
{acquired by purchase or lease) or into an existing City-owned facility, yet to be determined. 
Depending on the preferred and most cost effective option, other City Departments may be 
displaced during an interim period until the administrative building is completed. 
In the Spring of 2016, the City issued a Request for Proposals {"RFP") for a real estate 
brokerage firm to provide representation in the acquisition process, and has since selected a finn for 
this purpose. The brokerage firm has requested that the City acknowledge the possibility of dual 
representation; where it would represent both the buyer and seller, or lessee and lessor, in the 
transaction. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[ m ]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stiga/1 v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is 
intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of 
impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section I 090 is void. (Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) 


File No. A-16-173 
Page No. 3 
For Section I 090 to apply, however, there must be a public actor who is subject to the law 
and who has a financial interest in a contract he or she makes in his or her official capacity. Courts 
have found independent contractors serving in advisory positions that have a potential to exert 
considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency are subject to Section 
I 090. (See Hub City Solid Waste Sen•ices, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 
1124-1125; Schaefer v. Beri11stei11 ( 1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278 ["A person merely in an advisory 
position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest rule"]; City Council v. McKinley, ( 1978) 80 
Cal. App. 3d 204, 210-211 [ for Section 1090 to apply, the tenure of office must not be transient, 
occasional, or incidental, and that the officer has been delegated some portion of the sovereign 
functions of the public body].) 
While it is clear that Section I 090 applies to independent contractors as well as corporate 
consultants, the contractor or consultant must, at the very least, be advising or influencing the 
public body. ( 46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 ( 1965); see also Davis v. Fresno Unified School District 
(2015) 23 7 Cal.App.4th 261, 300 [ with respect to civil actions, the term "employees" in Section 
1090 applies to consultants, including corporate consultants, hired by local government agencies].) 
Based on your facts, however, there is no such public actor. The City has sought out the 
services of a brokerage firm for the limited purpose of facilitating the potential purchase, sale, and 
lease of commercial office space. Neither the real estate brokerage firm nor its employees serve in 
an advisory position or exert influence over the City or its decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

property transactions?

contract under which it now seeks to provide technical expertise in facilitating the acquisition or 
sale of real property, Section 1090 is not applicable.

who will represent the city as both the potential buyer and seller (or lessee and lessor) in real 
property transactions?

CONCLUSION: No. Where a real estate broker has not advised or influenced the public body prior to the 
contract under which it now seeks to provide technical expertise in facilitating the acquisition or 
sale of real property, Section 1090 is not applicable.
